blurb: Eight original olympiad-style algebra problems spanning minimization by AM-GM and Cauchy, quadratic-in-disguise substitutions, telescoping sums, reciprocal-substitution recurrences, trigonometric substitution in cubics, cyclic systems, and Newton's power-sum identities. Each rewards a structural insight rather than mechanical computation, and every closed-form answer has been verified symbolically and numerically.

problems.statement: For \(x>0\), find the minimum value of \[f(x)=x+\frac{4}{x^{2}}.\]
problems.solution: The trick is to split \(x\) into two equal pieces so that AM-GM can be applied to three terms whose product is a constant. Write \[f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{x}{2}+\frac{4}{x^{2}}.\] These three positive numbers have product \[\frac{x}{2}\cdot\frac{x}{2}\cdot\frac{4}{x^{2}}=1,\] which is independent of \(x\). By AM-GM, \[\frac{x}{2}+\frac{x}{2}+\frac{4}{x^{2}}\ge 3\sqrt[3]{\frac{x}{2}\cdot\frac{x}{2}\cdot\frac{4}{x^{2}}}=3\sqrt[3]{1}=3.\] Equality requires all three terms equal: \(\frac{x}{2}=\frac{4}{x^{2}}\), i.e. \(x^{3}=8\), so \(x=2\). Then \(f(2)=2+\frac{4}{4}=3\). Hence \(\min f=3\).
problems.method2: Calculus: \(f'(x)=1-\frac{8}{x^{3}}=0\) gives \(x^{3}=8\), so \(x=2\). Since \(f''(x)=\frac{24}{x^{4}}>0\), this is a minimum, and \(f(2)=3\). The point of the AM-GM method is that splitting \(x\) into the right number of equal pieces is exactly what makes the geometric mean constant.
problems.verify_note: sympy confirmed: f'(x)=0 has unique positive root x=2, f(2)=3 is the minimum.

problems.statement: Find all real solutions of \[\bigl(x^{2}-3x\bigr)^{2}-2\bigl(x^{2}-3x\bigr)=8.\]
problems.solution: Set \(u=x^{2}-3x\). The equation becomes \[u^{2}-2u-8=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad (u-4)(u+2)=0,\] so \(u=4\) or \(u=-2\). Now resolve each back in \(x\). For \(u=4\): \(x^{2}-3x-4=0\Rightarrow (x-4)(x+1)=0\Rightarrow x=4\) or \(x=-1\). For \(u=-2\): \(x^{2}-3x+2=0\Rightarrow (x-1)(x-2)=0\Rightarrow x=1\) or \(x=2\). All four values are real, so the solution set is \(\{-1,1,2,4\}\).
problems.method2: Direct expansion gives the quartic \(x^{4}-6x^{3}+7x^{2}+6x-8=0\), which factors as \((x+1)(x-1)(x-2)(x-4)=0\), recovering the same four roots. The substitution simply reveals this factorization without brute-force quartic work.
problems.verify_note: sympy confirmed: real solutions are exactly {-1,1,2,4}; expansion factors as (x+1)(x-1)(x-2)(x-4).

problems.statement: For a positive integer \(n\), evaluate in closed form \[S_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k}{(k+1)!}.\]
problems.solution: The key is to write the numerator as a difference that telescopes against the factorial. Since \(k=(k+1)-1\), \[\frac{k}{(k+1)!}=\frac{(k+1)-1}{(k+1)!}=\frac{k+1}{(k+1)!}-\frac{1}{(k+1)!}=\frac{1}{k!}-\frac{1}{(k+1)!}.\] Therefore the sum telescopes: \[S_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\!\left(\frac{1}{k!}-\frac{1}{(k+1)!}\right)=\frac{1}{1!}-\frac{1}{(n+1)!}=1-\frac{1}{(n+1)!}.\] As a sanity check, \(n=1\) gives \(\frac{1}{2}=1-\frac{1}{2}\); \(n=2\) gives \(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{6}=\frac{5}{6}=1-\frac{1}{6}\).
problems.method2: Guess-and-induct: from small cases \(S_1=\tfrac12,\ S_2=\tfrac56,\ S_3=\tfrac{23}{24}\) one conjectures \(S_n=1-\frac{1}{(n+1)!}\). For the inductive step, \(S_{n+1}=S_n+\frac{n+1}{(n+2)!}=1-\frac{1}{(n+1)!}+\frac{n+1}{(n+2)!}=1-\frac{(n+2)-(n+1)}{(n+2)!}=1-\frac{1}{(n+2)!}\), completing the induction.

problems.statement: Let \(a,b,c\) be positive reals with \(a+b+c=1\). Determine the maximum possible value of \[\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{b}+\sqrt{c}.\]
problems.solution: By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality applied to the vectors \((\sqrt a,\sqrt b,\sqrt c)\) and \((1,1,1)\), \[\bigl(\sqrt a+\sqrt b+\sqrt c\bigr)^{2}\le (a+b+c)\,(1^{2}+1^{2}+1^{2})=1\cdot 3=3.\] Hence \(\sqrt a+\sqrt b+\sqrt c\le\sqrt 3\). Equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds when \((\sqrt a,\sqrt b,\sqrt c)\) is proportional to \((1,1,1)\), i.e. \(a=b=c=\tfrac13\); then the sum is \(3\sqrt{1/3}=\sqrt 3\). So the maximum is \(\sqrt 3\). (Note there is no minimum over the open region: pushing two variables toward \(0\) drives the sum toward \(1\), but \(1\) is never attained.)
problems.method2: Jensen / concavity: \(g(t)=\sqrt t\) is concave on \([0,\infty)\), so \[\frac{\sqrt a+\sqrt b+\sqrt c}{3}\le\sqrt{\frac{a+b+c}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac13},\] giving \(\sqrt a+\sqrt b+\sqrt c\le 3\sqrt{1/3}=\sqrt 3\), with equality at \(a=b=c=\tfrac13\).
problems.verify_note: 200k-point random sampling on the simplex gave max approaching 1.73205 = sqrt(3); value at a=b=c=1/3 equals sqrt(3) exactly.

problems.statement: For \(x>0\), find the minimum value of \[F(x)=x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{1}{\,x^{2}+\dfrac{1}{x^{2}}\,}.\]
problems.solution: Substitute \(t=x^{2}+\dfrac{1}{x^{2}}\). By AM-GM, \(t=x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}\ge 2\), with equality iff \(x^{2}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}\), i.e. \(x=1\). The expression becomes \[F=g(t)=t+\frac{1}{t},\qquad t\ge 2.\] Here is the trap: the unconstrained minimum of \(g(t)=t+\frac1t\) is at \(t=1\), but \(t=1\) is not attainable since \(t\ge2\). On \([2,\infty)\) we have \(g'(t)=1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}>0\), so \(g\) is strictly increasing there; its minimum is therefore at the left endpoint \(t=2\): \[g(2)=2+\frac12=\frac52.\] This is achieved exactly when \(t=2\), i.e. \(x=1\). Hence \(\min F=\dfrac52\).
problems.method2: Direct AM-GM is invalid here because applying \(t+\frac1t\ge2\) would suggest a minimum of \(2\), which is unattainable given \(t\ge2\); recognizing this is the whole point. To confirm with calculus, at \(x=1\), \(F(1)=1+1+\frac{1}{2}=\frac52\), and a check of \(F'(x)\) shows \(x=1\) is the only interior critical point and yields the global minimum on \((0,\infty)\) (with \(F\to\infty\) as \(x\to0^+\) or \(x\to\infty\)).
problems.verify_note: sympy: unique positive critical point x=1 with F(1)=5/2; numeric grid scan over (0.2,5) gives min 2.5000 at x=1, confirming the monotonicity-trap reasoning.

problems.statement: A sequence is defined by \(a_1=\dfrac12\) and \[a_{n+1}=\frac{a_n}{1+3a_n}\quad(n\ge1).\] Find a closed form for \(a_n\), and evaluate \(\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k a_{k+1}\).
problems.solution: Each term is positive, so we may take reciprocals. Let \(b_n=\dfrac{1}{a_n}\). Then \[b_{n+1}=\frac{1+3a_n}{a_n}=\frac{1}{a_n}+3=b_n+3.\] Thus \((b_n)\) is an arithmetic progression with first term \(b_1=\frac{1}{a_1}=2\) and common difference \(3\): \(b_n=2+3(n-1)=3n-1\). Hence \[a_n=\frac{1}{3n-1}.\] For the sum, use partial fractions: \[a_k a_{k+1}=\frac{1}{(3k-1)(3k+2)}=\frac{1}{3}\!\left(\frac{1}{3k-1}-\frac{1}{3k+2}\right).\] This telescopes: \[\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k a_{k+1}=\frac13\!\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3n+2}\right)=\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{3n}{2(3n+2)}=\frac{n}{2(3n+2)}.\]
problems.method2: One can verify the closed form by induction: it holds at \(n=1\) since \(a_1=\frac12=\frac{1}{3\cdot1-1}\), and if \(a_n=\frac{1}{3n-1}\) then \(a_{n+1}=\frac{1/(3n-1)}{1+3/(3n-1)}=\frac{1}{(3n-1)+3}=\frac{1}{3(n+1)-1}\). The reciprocal substitution is what reveals this form in the first place, turning a nonlinear recurrence into a linear (arithmetic) one.
problems.verify_note: sympy: iterating the recurrence gives a_n=1/(3n-1) for n=1..7; sympy summation of 1/((3k-1)(3k+2)) equals n/(2(3n+2)) exactly.

problems.statement: Find all real roots of \[8x^{3}-6x+1=0,\] expressing them in closed trigonometric form, and prove that \[\cos40^{\circ}\cos80^{\circ}\cos160^{\circ}=-\frac18.\]
problems.answer: Roots: \(x=\cos40^{\circ},\ \cos80^{\circ},\ \cos160^{\circ}\); and the product equals \(-\tfrac18\). \(\text{(}=\)Proof for the identity.\()\)
problems.solution: Substitute \(x=\cos\theta\). Using the triple-angle identity \(\cos3\theta=4\cos^{3}\theta-3\cos\theta\), we have \(4x^{3}-3x=\cos3\theta\). Divide the equation by \(2\): \[4x^{3}-3x=-\tfrac12\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \cos3\theta=-\tfrac12.\] Thus \(3\theta=120^{\circ}+360^{\circ}k\) or \(3\theta=240^{\circ}+360^{\circ}k\), giving \(\theta\in\{40^{\circ},80^{\circ},160^{\circ}\}\) (the three values in \([0^{\circ},180^{\circ}]\) yielding distinct cosines). Hence the three real roots are \[x=\cos40^{\circ},\ \cos80^{\circ},\ \cos160^{\circ},\] and since a cubic has at most three roots, these are all of them. Now apply Vieta's formulas to \(8x^{3}+0\cdot x^{2}-6x+1=0\): the product of the roots is \(-\dfrac{\text{constant}}{\text{leading}}=-\dfrac{1}{8}\). Therefore \[\cos40^{\circ}\cos80^{\circ}\cos160^{\circ}=-\frac18.\]
problems.method2: Independent proof of the product identity via the doubling telescope. Let \(P=\cos40^{\circ}\cos80^{\circ}\cos160^{\circ}\). Multiply by \(\sin40^{\circ}\) and use \(2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha=\sin2\alpha\) repeatedly: \(8\sin40^{\circ}\,P = 8\sin40^{\circ}\cos40^{\circ}\cos80^{\circ}\cos160^{\circ}=4\sin80^{\circ}\cos80^{\circ}\cos160^{\circ}=2\sin160^{\circ}\cos160^{\circ}=\sin320^{\circ}=-\sin40^{\circ}.\) Since \(\sin40^{\circ}\neq0\), \(8P=-1\), so \(P=-\tfrac18\), confirming the Vieta computation.
problems.verify_note: sympy: roots of 8x^3-6x+1 are numerically {-0.93969, 0.17365, 0.76604} = {cos160,cos80,cos40}; cos(3*40)=cos(3*80)=cos(3*160)=-1/2; product simplifies symbolically to exactly -1/8 (sp.simplify(prod+1/8)=0).

problems.statement: Real numbers \(x,y,z\) satisfy \[x+y+z=2,\qquad x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=6,\qquad x^{3}+y^{3}+z^{3}=8.\] Find the unordered triple \(\{x,y,z\}\) and the value of \(x^{4}+y^{4}+z^{4}\).
problems.solution: Let \(p_k=x^k+y^k+z^k\) and let \(e_1,e_2,e_3\) be the elementary symmetric polynomials, so \(x,y,z\) are the roots of \[t^{3}-e_1 t^{2}+e_2 t-e_3=0.\] We are given \(p_1=2,\ p_2=6,\ p_3=8\). \emph{Newton's identities} give \(e_1,e_2,e_3\): \[e_1=p_1=2,\] \[e_2=\frac{e_1 p_1-p_2}{2}=\frac{2\cdot2-6}{2}=-1,\] \[e_3=\frac{e_2 p_1-e_1 p_2+p_3}{3}=\frac{(-1)(2)-(2)(6)+8}{3}=\frac{-2-12+8}{3}=-2.\] Hence the cubic is \[t^{3}-2t^{2}-t+2=0.\] Factor by grouping: \(t^{2}(t-2)-(t-2)=(t-2)(t^{2}-1)=(t-2)(t-1)(t+1)=0,\) so the roots are \(t=-1,1,2\). Therefore \(\{x,y,z\}=\{-1,1,2\}\). Finally, Newton's identity for \(p_4\) (with three variables, \(e_4=0\)) reads \[p_4=e_1 p_3-e_2 p_2+e_3 p_1=2\cdot8-(-1)\cdot6+(-2)\cdot2=16+6-4=18.\] Directly, \((-1)^4+1^4+2^4=1+1+16=18\), confirming \(x^{4}+y^{4}+z^{4}=18\).
problems.method2: Avoid Newton's identities by hand: \(e_2=\tfrac12\bigl[(x+y+z)^2-(x^2+y^2+z^2)\bigr]=\tfrac12(4-6)=-1\). For \(e_3\), use the identity \(x^3+y^3+z^3-3xyz=(x+y+z)(x^2+y^2+z^2-xy-yz-zx)\): since \(xy+yz+zx=e_2=-1\), the right side is \(2\bigl(6-(-1)\bigr)=14\), so \(8-3e_3=14\Rightarrow e_3=-2\). This reproduces the cubic \(t^3-2t^2-t+2=0\) and the roots \(\{-1,1,2\}\), after which \(x^4+y^4+z^4=18\) follows by direct substitution.
problems.verify_note: sympy: Newton's identities give e1=2,e2=-1,e3=-2; cubic t^3-2t^2-t+2 has roots {-1,1,2}; these satisfy p1=2,p2=6,p3=8 and p4=18.

blurb: Eight original olympiad-style number theory problems spanning divisibility, modular arithmetic, multiplicative orders and primitive roots, p-adic valuation (Lifting the Exponent), and classic structure results. Each rewards a clean insight — periodicity towers, residue pairing, the order-divides-exponent lemma, LTE, and the smallest-prime-factor argument — rather than mechanical computation. Ordered easiest to hardest.

problems.statement: Find the last two digits of \(7^{\left(7^{7}\right)}\).
problems.solution:
The last two digits of a number are its residue modulo \(100\). We compute \(7^{(7^7)} \bmod 100\).
\par\textbf{Step 1: order of \(7\) modulo \(100\).} Since \(\gcd(7,100)=1\) and \(7^2 = 49\), \(7^4 = 49^2 = 2401 \equiv 1 \pmod{100}\). Thus the multiplicative order of \(7\) modulo \(100\) is \(4\) (it is not \(1\) or \(2\) since \(7^2\equiv 49\)). Therefore \(7^E \bmod 100\) depends only on \(E \bmod 4\).
\par\textbf{Step 2: reduce the exponent modulo \(4\).} We need \(E = 7^7 \bmod 4\). Since \(7 \equiv -1 \pmod 4\), we get \(7^7 \equiv (-1)^7 = -1 \equiv 3 \pmod 4\).
\par\textbf{Step 3: assemble.} Hence \(7^{(7^7)} \equiv 7^{3} \pmod{100}\). Now \(7^3 = 343 \equiv 43 \pmod{100}\).
\par The last two digits are \(\boxed{43}\).
problems.method2: Use the Carmichael/Euler structure directly. Modulo \(4\): \(7\equiv 3\), and \(7^k\bmod 4\) cycles with period \(2\) as \(3,1,3,1,\dots\), so since \(7\) is odd, \(7^{(7^7)}\equiv 3 \equiv -1 \pmod 4\). Modulo \(25\): \(\lambda(25)=20\) and \(7^4 = 2401 = 96\cdot 25 + 1\equiv 1\pmod{25}\), so again only \(E\bmod 4\) matters, giving \(7^3 = 343 \equiv 343-325 = 18 \pmod{25}\). Solve \(x\equiv -1\pmod 4,\ x\equiv 18\pmod{25}\) by CRT: \(x=43\) works (\(43\equiv 3\pmod4\), \(43\equiv 18\pmod{25}\)), giving \(43\).
problems.verify_note: sympy: pow(7,7**7,100)=43 confirmed.

problems.statement: Determine the largest integer \(k\) such that \(12^{k}\) divides \(100!\).
problems.solution:
Write \(12 = 2^2 \cdot 3\). Then \(12^k = 2^{2k}\cdot 3^{k}\) divides \(100!\) iff \(2k \le v_2(100!)\) and \(k \le v_3(100!)\), where \(v_p\) denotes the exponent of the prime \(p\).
\par\textbf{Legendre's formula} gives \(v_p(n!) = \sum_{i\ge 1}\lfloor n/p^i\rfloor\).
\par For \(p=2\): \(v_2(100!) = 50 + 25 + 12 + 6 + 3 + 1 = 97\).
\par For \(p=3\): \(v_3(100!) = 33 + 11 + 3 + 1 = 48\).
\par The two constraints are \(2k \le 97\) (i.e. \(k \le 48\), since \(k\) is an integer and \(\lfloor 97/2\rfloor = 48\)) and \(k \le 48\). The binding (smaller) bound is \(k\le 48\) from the prime \(3\).
\par Therefore the largest such \(k\) is \(\boxed{48}\). The insight is that although \(2\) is far more abundant than \(3\), the \(2^2\) per copy of \(12\) means the supply of \(2\)'s suffices for \(48\) copies (\(2\cdot 48 = 96 \le 97\)); the genuine bottleneck is the prime \(3\).
problems.method2: Compare supplies as ratios. Each \(12\) consumes two \(2\)'s and one \(3\). The number of available \(2\)-pairs is \(\lfloor 97/2\rfloor = 48\); the number of available \(3\)'s is \(48\). The achievable count is the minimum, \(\min(48,48)=48\).
problems.verify_note: sympy factorint(100!): v2=97, v3=48, min(97//2,48)=48 confirmed.

problems.statement: Find all positive integers \(n\) such that \(n^{2} \mid 2^{n} + 1\).
problems.solution:
Clearly \(n=1\) works (\(1\mid 3\)) and \(n=3\) works (\(9 \mid 9\)). We show these are the only solutions.
\par Suppose \(n^2 \mid 2^n + 1\) with \(n > 1\). Since \(2^n+1\) is odd, \(n\) is odd. Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(n\); then \(p\) is odd.
\par\textbf{Step 1: \(p = 3\).} From \(p \mid 2^n + 1\) we get \(2^n \equiv -1 \pmod p\), hence \(2^{2n} \equiv 1 \pmod p\). Let \(d = \operatorname{ord}_p(2)\). Then \(d \mid 2n\) but \(d \nmid n\) (else \(2^n\equiv 1\), contradicting \(2^n\equiv -1\) for odd \(p\)). Also \(d \mid p-1\) by Fermat. Now \(d\mid 2n\) and \(d\nmid n\) force \(v_2(d) = v_2(2n) = 1\), so \(d = 2m\) with \(m \mid n\) odd. Since \(m \mid n\) and \(m \mid d \mid p-1 < p\), every prime factor of \(m\) is a prime factor of \(n\) that is \(< p\); by minimality of \(p\), \(m\) has no prime factors, i.e. \(m=1\) and \(d = 2\). Then \(2^2 \equiv 1 \pmod p\), so \(p \mid 3\), giving \(p = 3\). Hence \(3 \mid n\).
\par\textbf{Step 2: bound the power of \(3\) via LTE.} Write \(a = v_3(n) \ge 1\). Since \(n\) is odd, by the Lifting the Exponent lemma for \(p=3\),
\[ v_3(2^n + 1) = v_3(2+1) + v_3(n) = 1 + a. \]
The hypothesis \(n^2 \mid 2^n+1\) requires \(v_3(n^2) \le v_3(2^n+1)\), i.e. \(2a \le 1 + a\), so \(a \le 1\). Thus \(a = 1\): exactly one factor of \(3\) divides \(n\).
\par\textbf{Step 3: no other prime divides \(n\).} Suppose \(n > 3\). Write \(n = 3m\) with \(m > 1\) and \(\gcd(m,3)=1\) (by Step 2). Let \(q\) be the smallest prime factor of \(m\); then \(q > 3\), and \(q\) is the smallest prime factor of \(n\) other than \(3\). From \(q \mid 2^n + 1\), repeat the order analysis: \(e := \operatorname{ord}_q(2)\) satisfies \(e \mid 2n\), \(e \nmid n\), so \(e = 2t\) with \(t\) odd, \(t \mid n\). Each prime factor of \(t\) divides \(n\) and is \(\le t \mid e \mid q-1 < q\), so it is a prime factor of \(n\) smaller than \(q\) — hence it must be \(3\). Thus \(t \in \{1, 3\}\) (as \(v_3(n)=1\)), giving \(e \in \{2, 6\}\), so \(q \mid 2^2 - 1 = 3\) or \(q \mid 2^6 - 1 = 63 = 3^2\cdot 7\). The only prime \(>3\) here is \(q = 7\). But \(e=6\) and \(7\mid 2^n+1\) would need \(2^n\equiv -1\pmod 7\); the powers of \(2\) mod \(7\) cycle \(2,4,1\) and never equal \(6\equiv -1\). Contradiction. Hence no such \(q\) exists and \(m=1\).
\par Therefore \(n = 3\), and the complete solution set is \(\boxed{\{1, 3\}}\).
problems.method2: After Step 1 establishes \(3\mid n\) and Step 2 (LTE) forces \(v_3(n)=1\), one can finish by a clean lifting argument: assume for contradiction \(n>3\) and take the second-smallest prime \(q\mid n\). Since \(2^{2n}\equiv 1\pmod q\) but \(2^n\equiv -1\), the order of \(2\) mod \(q\) is even, equal to \(2t\) with \(t\mid n\); minimality forces \(t\) to be built only from the already-found prime \(3\), so \(\operatorname{ord}_q(2)\in\{2,6\}\) and \(q\mid 2^6-1\). Checking \(q=7\) directly fails since \(-1\) is not a power of \(2\) mod \(7\). This contradiction gives \(n=3\).
problems.verify_note: Brute force search n up to 200000 found exactly {1,3}; also verified 2^k mod 7 cycles 2,4,1 (never 6) confirming the Step 3 contradiction.

problems.statement: Let \(p\) be an odd prime. Prove that the product of all primitive roots modulo \(p\) (taken among the residues \(1,2,\dots,p-1\)) is congruent to \(1 \pmod p\) when \(p > 3\), and to \(-1 \pmod 3\) when \(p = 3\).
problems.solution:
For \(p = 3\), the only primitive root is \(2\), and the product is \(2 \equiv -1 \pmod 3\). Now assume \(p > 3\).
\par\textbf{Setup.} The group \((\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\) is cyclic of order \(p-1\); fix a generator \(g\). Every element is \(g^k\) for a unique \(k \in \{0,1,\dots,p-2\}\), and \(g^k\) is a primitive root iff \(\gcd(k, p-1) = 1\). There are \(\varphi(p-1)\) primitive roots, indexed by the set \(U = \{k : 1 \le k \le p-1,\ \gcd(k,p-1)=1\}\).
\par\textbf{Product of indices.} The product of all primitive roots is
\[ P \equiv \prod_{k \in U} g^{k} = g^{\,S}, \qquad S = \sum_{k\in U} k. \]
\par\textbf{Symmetry of the exponent sum.} The set \(U\) is symmetric under \(k \mapsto (p-1)-k\): indeed \(\gcd(k, p-1) = \gcd((p-1)-k,\, p-1)\). Since \(p > 3\), we have \(p - 1 > 2\), so no element is fixed by this involution (a fixed point would need \(k = (p-1)/2\), but then \(\gcd(k,p-1) = (p-1)/2 > 1\), so it is not in \(U\)). Hence \(U\) splits into \(\tfrac{1}{2}\varphi(p-1)\) pairs \(\{k, (p-1)-k\}\), each summing to \(p-1\). Therefore
\[ S = \frac{\varphi(p-1)}{2}\,(p-1). \]
\par\textbf{Conclusion.} Then \(P \equiv g^{S} = \left(g^{\,p-1}\right)^{\varphi(p-1)/2} \equiv 1^{\varphi(p-1)/2} = 1 \pmod p\), since \(g^{p-1}\equiv 1\) by Fermat. (Note \(\varphi(p-1)\) is even for \(p-1>2\), so \(S\) is an integer multiple of \(p-1\).) This proves \(P \equiv 1 \pmod p\) for \(p > 3\). \(\blacksquare\)
problems.method2: Pair each primitive root with its inverse. If \(r\) is a primitive root then so is \(r^{-1}\), and \(r\cdot r^{-1}\equiv 1\). A root is its own inverse only if \(r^2\equiv 1\), i.e. \(r\equiv \pm 1\); but \(1\) has order \(1\) and \(-1\) has order \(2\), neither equal to \(p-1\) when \(p>3\). Hence the primitive roots pair off into self-inverse-free pairs whose product is \(1\), so the total product is \(1 \pmod p\). For \(p=3\), \(p-1=2\) and the lone primitive root \(2\equiv -1\) is its own inverse, giving \(-1\).
problems.verify_note: Computationally confirmed: product of primitive roots mod p equals 1 for p in {5,7,11,13,17,19,23,29,31} and equals 2(=-1) for p=3; also verified no primitive root is self-inverse for p>3, validating method2.

problems.statement: The number \(2027\) is prime. Find the remainder when \[ 1^{2025} + 2^{2025} + 3^{2025} + \cdots + 2026^{2025} \] is divided by \(2027\).
problems.solution:
Let \(p = 2027\) (prime) and \(k = 2025\); note \(p - 1 = 2026\). We evaluate \(S = \sum_{a=1}^{p-1} a^{k} \bmod p\).
\par\textbf{Key lemma.} For a prime \(p\) and integer \(k \ge 1\),
\[ \sum_{a=1}^{p-1} a^{k} \equiv \begin{cases} -1 \pmod p, & (p-1)\mid k,\\[2pt] \;\;0 \pmod p, & (p-1)\nmid k. \end{cases} \]
\par\textbf{Proof of lemma.} Let \(g\) be a primitive root mod \(p\). As \(a\) ranges over \(1,\dots,p-1\), the residue \(a\) ranges over \(g^0, g^1, \dots, g^{p-2}\) in some order. Hence
\[ S \equiv \sum_{j=0}^{p-2} (g^{j})^{k} = \sum_{j=0}^{p-2} (g^{k})^{j}. \]
This is a geometric series with ratio \(t = g^{k}\). If \((p-1)\mid k\) then \(t \equiv 1\) and the sum is \(p-1 \equiv -1\). If \((p-1)\nmid k\) then \(t \not\equiv 1\) (since \(g\) has order \(p-1\)), and
\[ S \equiv \frac{t^{\,p-1} - 1}{t - 1} \equiv \frac{1 - 1}{t-1} = 0 \pmod p, \]
using \(t^{p-1} = g^{k(p-1)} \equiv 1\) by Fermat. \(\square\)
\par\textbf{Apply.} Here \(p - 1 = 2026\) and \(k = 2025\). Since \(2026 \nmid 2025\) (indeed \(0 < 2025 < 2026\)), we are in the second case, so \(S \equiv 0 \pmod{2027}\).
\par The remainder is \(\boxed{0}\).
problems.method2: Pair \(a\) with \(p-a\). Since \(k=2025\) is odd, \((p-a)^{k}\equiv (-a)^{k} = -a^{k}\pmod p\). Thus \(a^{k} + (p-a)^{k} \equiv 0\), and the \(p-1 = 2026\) terms split into \(1013\) such pairs (no term is self-paired since \(p\) is odd, so \(a \ne p-a\)). Each pair contributes \(0\), giving total \(0 \pmod{2027}\). This pairing argument needs only that the exponent is odd.
problems.verify_note: sympy: sum_{a=1}^{2026} a^2025 mod 2027 = 0, and 2027 confirmed prime.

problems.statement: Determine the largest integer \(N\) such that \(3^{N}\) divides \(2^{\left(3^{7}\right)} + 1\).
problems.solution:
We must compute \(v_3\!\left(2^{3^7} + 1\right)\), the exponent of \(3\) in the factorization.
\par\textbf{LTE for sums.} The Lifting the Exponent lemma states: for an odd prime \(p\) with \(p \mid a + b\) and \(p \nmid a,\,p\nmid b\), and for an odd positive integer \(m\),
\[ v_p\!\left(a^{m} + b^{m}\right) = v_p(a+b) + v_p(m). \]
\par\textbf{Apply with \(a = 2,\ b = 1,\ p = 3,\ m = 3^7\).} Here \(3 \mid 2 + 1 = 3\), \(3 \nmid 2\), \(3\nmid 1\), and \(m = 3^7\) is odd. Hence
\[ v_3\!\left(2^{3^7} + 1\right) = v_3(2 + 1) + v_3\!\left(3^7\right) = v_3(3) + 7 = 1 + 7 = 8. \]
\par Therefore \(3^{8}\) divides \(2^{3^7}+1\) but \(3^{9}\) does not, and the answer is \(N = \boxed{8}\).
problems.method2: Prove the increment by induction without quoting LTE. Let \(x_k = 2^{3^k}+1\). Then \(x_{k+1} = 2^{3^{k+1}}+1 = \left(2^{3^k}\right)^3 + 1 = (x_k - 1)^3 + 1\). Expanding, \(x_{k+1} = x_k\big((x_k-1)^2 - (x_k-1) + 1\big) = x_k\,(x_k^2 - 3x_k + 3)\). Since \(3 \mid x_k\) (as \(x_0 = 3\) and the recursion preserves divisibility by \(3\)), the factor \(x_k^2 - 3x_k + 3\) is \(\equiv 3 \pmod 9\) (each of \(x_k^2\) and \(3x_k\) is divisible by \(9\) for \(k\ge 1\), leaving \(3\)), so \(v_3\) of that factor is exactly \(1\). Thus \(v_3(x_{k+1}) = v_3(x_k) + 1\). With \(v_3(x_0) = v_3(3) = 1\), induction gives \(v_3(x_k) = k+1\); for \(k = 7\) this is \(8\).
problems.verify_note: Direct computation: v3(2^(3^7)+1)=8; also verified the recursion v3(x_k)=k+1 for k=0..7.

problems.statement: Consider \(F = 2^{32} + 1 = 4294967297\). Prove that every prime divisor \(p\) of \(F\) satisfies \(p \equiv 1 \pmod{64}\), and use this to find the smallest prime divisor of \(F\).
problems.solution:
extbf{Step 1: a congruence for prime divisors.} Let \(p\) be a prime with \(p \mid 2^{32} + 1\). Then \(2^{32} \equiv -1 \pmod p\), so squaring gives \(2^{64} \equiv 1 \pmod p\). Let \(d = \operatorname{ord}_p(2)\). Then \(d \mid 64\), so \(d\) is a power of \(2\). But \(d 
mid 32\): if \(d \mid 32\) we would have \(2^{32}\equiv 1\), contradicting \(2^{32}\equiv -1 \pmod p\) (these differ since \(p\) is odd, \(p 
mid 2\)). The only divisor of \(64\) that does not divide \(32\) is \(64\) itself, so \(d = 64\).
\par By Fermat's little theorem \(d \mid p - 1\), hence \(64 \mid p - 1\), i.e. \(p \equiv 1 \pmod{64}\). This proves the claim for every prime divisor.
\par	extbf{Step 2: search the admissible candidates.} A prime factor must have the form \(p = 64k + 1\). Testing the primes of this form in increasing order:
\par \(65 = 5\cdot 13\) (not prime); \(129 = 3\cdot 43\) (not prime); \(193\) prime — \(193 
mid F\); \(257\) prime — \(257 
mid F\); \(321 = 3\cdot 107,\ 385 = 5\cdot 7\cdot 11\) (not prime); \(449\) prime — \(449 
mid F\); \(513 = 27\cdot 19\) (not prime); \(577\) prime — \(577 
mid F\); \(641\) prime — and \(641 \mid F\).
\par Indeed \(F = 4294967297 = 641 \cdot 6700417\), so the smallest prime divisor is \( oxed{641}\).
\par (One can confirm \(641 \mid 2^{32}+1\) elegantly: \(641 = 5^4 + 2^4 = 5\cdot 2^7 + 1\). From \(5\cdot 2^7 \equiv -1\) we get \(5^4 \cdot 2^{28} \equiv 1\); and from \(5^4 \equiv -2^4\) we get \(-2^{4}\cdot 2^{28} = -2^{32} \equiv 1\), i.e. \(2^{32} \equiv -1 \pmod{641}\).)
problems.method2: The slick divisibility check by Euler/Fermat's identity: \(641 = 2^4 + 5^4 = 1 + 5\cdot 2^7\). The second form gives \(5\cdot 2^7 \equiv -1\pmod{641}\), so \((5\cdot 2^7)^4 \equiv 1\), i.e. \(5^4\cdot 2^{28}\equiv 1\). The first form gives \(5^4 \equiv -2^4 \pmod{641}\). Substituting, \(-2^4\cdot 2^{28} = -2^{32}\equiv 1\), hence \(2^{32}\equiv -1\) and \(641 \mid 2^{32}+1\). Combined with Step 1's lower bound (any prime factor is at least \(193\), and one checks \(193,257,449,577\) fail), \(641\) is the least prime factor.
problems.verify_note: sympy: factorint(2^32+1)={641,6700417}; 641 prime, 641|F, 641 mod 64=1; verified candidates 193,257,449,577 all fail to divide F, confirming 641 is smallest.

problems.statement: Let \(a \ge 2\) be an integer. Prove that for every integer \(n \ge 1\), \[ n \,\bigm|\, \varphi\!\left(a^{n} - 1\right), \] where \(\varphi\) is Euler's totient function.
problems.solution:
Set \(m = a^{n} - 1\). For \(n = 1\), \(\varphi(a-1)\) is divisible by \(1\) trivially, so assume \(n \ge 2\); then \(m = a^n - 1 \ge a^2 - 1 \ge 3\).
\par\textbf{Step 1: \(a\) is a unit modulo \(m\).} Since \(a^{n} \equiv 1 \pmod m\) by construction (\(m \mid a^n - 1\)), \(a\) is invertible modulo \(m\); equivalently \(\gcd(a, m) = 1\) (any common prime factor would divide \(a^n\) and \(a^n - 1\), hence \(1\)). So \(a\) lies in the multiplicative group \((\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z})^\times\), which has order \(\varphi(m)\).
\par\textbf{Step 2: the order of \(a\) modulo \(m\) is exactly \(n\).} Let \(d = \operatorname{ord}_m(a)\). From \(a^n \equiv 1\) we get \(d \mid n\). Conversely, suppose \(d < n\). Then \(a^{d} \equiv 1 \pmod m\) means \(m \mid a^{d} - 1\), so
\[ a^{n} - 1 = m \le a^{d} - 1. \]
But \(a \ge 2\) and \(d < n\) give \(a^{d} - 1 < a^{n} - 1\), a strict inequality, contradicting \(a^n - 1 \le a^d - 1\). (We used that \(a^d - 1 \ge 1 > 0\), so divisibility forces \(m \le a^d - 1\).) Hence no \(d < n\) works, and \(d = n\).
\par\textbf{Step 3: Lagrange / Euler.} The order of any element of a finite group divides the group's order (Lagrange); equivalently, \(\operatorname{ord}_m(a) \mid \varphi(m)\) by Euler's theorem (\(a^{\varphi(m)}\equiv 1\)). Therefore
\[ n = \operatorname{ord}_m(a) \,\bigm|\, \varphi(m) = \varphi(a^{n} - 1). \]
\par This holds for all \(n \ge 1\) and all \(a \ge 2\). \(\blacksquare\)
\par\textbf{Remark.} The crux is recognizing that \(a^n - 1\) is built precisely so that \(a\) has order exactly \(n\) modulo it — minimality of the exponent comes for free from the size comparison \(a^d - 1 < a^n - 1\). No factorization of \(a^n-1\) is needed.
problems.method2: Frame it via cyclotomic structure: \(a^n - 1 = \prod_{d \mid n}\Phi_d(a)\) where \(\Phi_d\) is the \(d\)-th cyclotomic polynomial. A prime \(p\) dividing \(\Phi_n(a)\) (and not dividing \(n\)) satisfies \(\operatorname{ord}_p(a) = n\), forcing \(n \mid p - 1\); the primitive prime divisor existence (Zsygmondy, for \(n \ge 2\), \((a,n)\ne(2,6)\)) then exhibits such a \(p\) with \(n \mid p-1 \mid \varphi(a^n-1)\). However the order argument above is cleaner and fully self-contained, handling all cases (including \((a,n)=(2,6)\)) uniformly, since it only needs that \(a\) itself has order \(n\) modulo \(a^n-1\), which is elementary.
problems.verify_note: Verified for a in 2..7, n in 1..11 (m>1): n | totient(a^n-1) holds throughout, and ord_m(a)=n confirmed in every case, validating Steps 2-3.

blurb: Eight original synthetic plane-geometry problems for olympiad training, moving from quick angle-chasing warm-ups to power-of-a-point, similarity, Ptolemy, and a coordinate/orthocenter finale. Each rewards a single clean insight rather than mechanical computation, and every numeric answer has been independently verified.

problems.statement: In triangle \(ABC\) we have \(AB = AC\) and \(\angle BAC = 40^\circ\). The point \(D\) lies on ray \(BC\) beyond \(C\) so that \(CD = CA\). Find \(\angle ADB\).
problems.solution:
Since \(AB=AC\), the base angles satisfy \(\angle ABC = \angle ACB = \tfrac{180^\circ-40^\circ}{2} = 70^\circ\).\par Now look at triangle \(ACD\). Because \(D\) lies on segment \(BC\) extended beyond \(C\), the angle \(\angle ACD\) is the supplement of \(\angle ACB\):
\[
\angle ACD = 180^\circ - 70^\circ = 110^\circ.
\]
We are given \(CD = CA\), so triangle \(ACD\) is isosceles with apex at \(C\). Its two equal base angles are
\[
\angle CAD = \angle ADC = \frac{180^\circ - 110^\circ}{2} = 35^\circ.
\]
Finally \(\angle ADB\) is the same angle as \(\angle ADC\) (since \(B\), \(C\), \(D\) are collinear and \(B\) lies on the \(C\)-side), so \(\angle ADB = 35^\circ\).
problems.method2: Exterior-angle view: in triangle \(ACD\), the angle \(\angle ACB = 70^\circ\) is exterior at \(C\), hence equals the sum of the two remote interior angles \(\angle CAD + \angle ADC\). With \(CA=CD\) those two are equal, so each is \(70^\circ/2 = 35^\circ\), giving \(\angle ADB = 35^\circ\) immediately.
problems.verify_note: sympy/coordinate check: built triangle with base angles 70 deg, placed D beyond C with CD=CA, measured angle ADB = 35.0 deg exactly.

problems.statement: A point \(P\) lies outside a circle \(\omega\). A line through \(P\) is tangent to \(\omega\) at \(T\). A second line through \(P\) meets \(\omega\) at \(A\) and \(B\), with \(A\) between \(P\) and \(B\); here \(PA = 4\) and \(AB = 5\). A third line through \(P\) meets \(\omega\) at \(C\) and \(D\), with \(C\) between \(P\) and \(D\), and \(PC = 3\). Find \(PT\) and \(CD\).
problems.solution:
The power of the point \(P\) with respect to \(\omega\) is a single number that every line through \(P\) must reproduce.\par Using the secant through \(A,B\):
\[
\operatorname{pow}(P) = PA\cdot PB = PA\,(PA+AB) = 4\cdot(4+5) = 4\cdot 9 = 36.
\]
For the tangent, \(\operatorname{pow}(P) = PT^2\), so
\[
PT = \sqrt{36} = 6.
\]
For the secant through \(C,D\):
\[
PC\cdot PD = 36 \;\Rightarrow\; PD = \frac{36}{3} = 12,
\]
hence \(CD = PD - PC = 12 - 3 = 9.\)
problems.method2: One can derive \(PT^2 = PA\cdot PB\) from scratch: triangles \(PTA\) and \(PBT\) are similar because \(\angle TPA = \angle BPT\) (shared) and \(\angle PTA = \angle PBT\) (tangent-chord angle equals the inscribed angle subtending \(TA\)). Then \(\tfrac{PT}{PB} = \tfrac{PA}{PT}\) gives \(PT^2 = PA\cdot PB = 36\), and the same similarity logic with the \(C,D\) secant fixes \(PD\).
problems.verify_note: sympy confirmed pow(P)=PA*PB=36, PT=sqrt(36)=6, PD=36/3=12, CD=12-3=9.

problems.statement: In right triangle \(ABC\) the right angle is at \(C\), and \(CA = 3\), \(CB = 4\). The foot of the altitude from \(C\) to the hypotenuse \(AB\) is \(H\). Let \(r_1\) be the inradius of triangle \(ACH\) and \(r_2\) the inradius of triangle \(BCH\). Find the ratio \(r_1 : r_2\), and prove it does not depend on the particular lengths but only on the legs.
problems.solution:
The altitude to the hypotenuse creates the classical chain of similar triangles
\[
\triangle ACH \sim \triangle ABC \sim \triangle CBH,
\]
because each shares an acute angle with \(\triangle ABC\) and each has a right angle. In particular \(\triangle ACH \sim \triangle CBH\).\par Now here is the key observation: the inradius is a length, and any length scales by the same factor under a similarity. So if \(\triangle ACH \sim \triangle CBH\) with ratio \(k\), then \(r_1/r_2 = k\). To find \(k\), compare corresponding hypotenuses: the hypotenuse of \(\triangle ACH\) is \(CA\), and the corresponding hypotenuse of \(\triangle CBH\) is \(CB\) (these are the sides opposite the right angles at \(H\)). Hence
\[
\frac{r_1}{r_2} = \frac{CA}{CB} = \frac{3}{4}.
\]
This depends only on the legs, as claimed.\par As a concrete check with \(CA=3, CB=4, AB=5\): the altitude is \(CH = \tfrac{CA\cdot CB}{AB} = \tfrac{12}{5}\), with \(AH = \tfrac{CA^2}{AB} = \tfrac{9}{5}\) and \(BH = \tfrac{CB^2}{AB} = \tfrac{16}{5}\). Using \(r = \tfrac{\text{leg}_1+\text{leg}_2-\text{hyp}}{2}\) for a right triangle,
\[
r_1 = \frac{CH + AH - CA}{2} = \frac{\tfrac{12}{5}+\tfrac{9}{5}-3}{2} = \frac{3}{5},\qquad r_2 = \frac{CH + BH - CB}{2} = \frac{\tfrac{12}{5}+\tfrac{16}{5}-4}{2} = \frac{4}{5},
\]
so \(r_1 : r_2 = \tfrac{3}{5} : \tfrac{4}{5} = 3 : 4\), matching the synthetic argument.
problems.method2: Avoid coordinates entirely: in any similar figures the inradius is proportional to a chosen homologous side. The similarities \(\triangle ACH \sim \triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle CBH \sim \triangle ABC\) have ratios \(CA/AB\) and \(CB/AB\) respectively (matching hypotenuse to hypotenuse). Since the inradius of \(\triangle ABC\) is a fixed length \(\rho\), we get \(r_1 = \rho\cdot CA/AB\) and \(r_2 = \rho\cdot CB/AB\), so \(r_1/r_2 = CA/CB\) directly.
problems.verify_note: Numeric check with CA=3,CB=4,AB=5: r1=3/5, r2=4/5, so r1/r2 = 3/4 exactly (sympy).

problems.statement: The incircle of triangle \(ABC\) touches side \(BC\) at \(X\), with \(BX = 4\) and \(CX = 6\). The tangent length from vertex \(A\) to the incircle equals \(5\) (that is, the incircle touches \(AB\) and \(AC\) at points whose distance to \(A\) is \(5\)). Find the area of triangle \(ABC\).
problems.solution:
Write the tangent lengths from the three vertices as \(x\) (from \(A\)), \(y\) (from \(B\)), \(z\) (from \(C\)). Equal tangents from an external point give
\[
y = BX = 4,\qquad z = CX = 6,\qquad x = 5 \;(\text{given}).
\]
The side lengths are then
\[
a = BC = y+z = 10,\quad b = CA = z+x = 11,\quad c = AB = x+y = 9.
\]
The semiperimeter is
\[
s = x+y+z = 5+4+6 = 15.
\]
(Notice the consistency \(s-a = 15-10 = 5 = x\), exactly the \(A\)-tangent length, as it must be.) By Heron's formula,
\[
[ABC] = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)} = \sqrt{15\cdot 5\cdot 4\cdot 6} = \sqrt{1800} = 30\sqrt{2}.
\]
problems.method2: Once \(r\) is found the area follows from \([ABC]=rs\). Here \(r = \sqrt{\tfrac{(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}{s}} = \sqrt{\tfrac{5\cdot 4\cdot 6}{15}} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt2\), so \([ABC] = rs = 2\sqrt2\cdot 15 = 30\sqrt2\).
problems.verify_note: sympy: Heron sqrt(15*5*4*6)=sqrt(1800)=30*sqrt(2); cross-check r=2*sqrt(2), rs=30*sqrt(2).

problems.statement: Let \(ABC\) be an equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle, and let \(P\) be a point on the arc \(BC\) not containing \(A\). Prove that \(PA = PB + PC\).
problems.solution:
Since \(P\) lies on the circumcircle of \(ABC\), the quadrilateral \(ABPC\) (vertices in this cyclic order \(A,B,P,C\) around the circle) is a cyclic quadrilateral. Apply Ptolemy's theorem, which states that for a cyclic quadrilateral the product of the diagonals equals the sum of the products of the two pairs of opposite sides:
\[
AP\cdot BC = AB\cdot PC + AC\cdot PB.
\]
Here \(AP\) and \(BC\) are the diagonals of \(ABPC\), while \(AB, PC\) and \(AC, PB\) are the two pairs of opposite sides.\par Because triangle \(ABC\) is equilateral, \(AB = AC = BC =: \ell\). Substituting,
\[
AP\cdot \ell = \ell\cdot PC + \ell\cdot PB.
\]
Dividing by \(\ell > 0\) yields
\[
PA = PB + PC,
\]
as required. \(\blacksquare\)\par (Equality, not merely \(\le\), is what makes this clean: Ptolemy gives an equation precisely because \(P\) is concyclic with \(A,B,C\); for a non-cyclic point the inequality \(PA \le PB+PC\) would hold instead.)
problems.method2: Trigonometric form. Let the circumradius be \(R\). For \(P\) on arc \(BC\), the inscribed angles give \(\angle BPA = \angle CPA = 60^\circ\) (each subtends a side of the equilateral triangle). By the extended law of sines in triangles \(ABP\) and \(ACP\), the chords are \(PB = 2R\sin\angle PAB\), \(PC = 2R\sin\angle PAC\), and \(PA = 2R\sin\angle ABP\). Using \(\angle PAB+\angle PAC = 60^\circ\) and the sum-to-product identity \(\sin u+\sin v = 2\sin\tfrac{u+v}{2}\cos\tfrac{u-v}{2}\) reduces \(PB+PC\) to exactly \(PA\). A direct coordinate check (side \(1\), \(P\) the arc midpoint) gives \(PA = PB+PC = \tfrac{2}{\sqrt3}\), confirming the identity.
problems.verify_note: Numeric: for many points P on arc BC of the circumcircle, PA-(PB+PC)=0 to 1e-9; arc-midpoint scaled to side 1 gives PA=2/sqrt(3)=1.1547, matching PB+PC.

problems.statement: Two circles \(\omega_1\) and \(\omega_2\) intersect at points \(P\) and \(Q\). A line through \(P\) meets \(\omega_1\) again at \(A\) and \(\omega_2\) again at \(B\). A second line through \(Q\) meets \(\omega_1\) again at \(C\) and \(\omega_2\) again at \(D\). Prove that \(AC \parallel BD\).
problems.solution:
We chase angles using the common chord \(PQ\), which links the two circles. Throughout we use directed angles between lines, taken modulo \(180^\circ\), so the argument is independent of how \(A,B,C,D\) happen to be arranged.\par Work in \(\omega_1\), which passes through \(A, C, P, Q\). Since these four points are concyclic, the inscribed-angle theorem for the chord \(CQ\) gives
\[
\angle (CA, AP) = \angle (CQ, QP),
\]
because \(\angle CAP\) (at \(A\)) and \(\angle CQP\) (at \(Q\)) are angles subtending the same chord \(CQ\) (no, \(CP\)) — more precisely, in directed-angle form, points \(A,C,P,Q\) concyclic means \(\angle(AC,AP)=\angle(QC,QP)\).\par Work in \(\omega_2\), through \(B, D, P, Q\). Likewise, concyclicity of \(B,D,P,Q\) gives
\[
\angle (BD, BP) = \angle (QD, QP).
\]
Now use the collinearities built into the construction. Points \(A, P, B\) lie on one line through \(P\), so line \(AP = \) line \(BP\). Points \(C, Q, D\) lie on one line through \(Q\), so line \(QC = \) line \(QD\). The second collinearity makes the two right-hand sides equal:
\[
\angle (QC, QP) = \angle (QD, QP).
\]
Chaining the three equalities,
\[
\angle (AC, AP) = \angle (BD, BP) = \angle (BD, AP),
\]
the last step using \(AP = BP\) as lines. Thus \(AC\) and \(BD\) make equal directed angles with the common line \(AP\). Equal directed angles with a common transversal mean the lines are parallel:
\[
AC \parallel BD. \qquad \blacksquare
\]
problems.method2: Undirected version, tracking supplements. In \(\omega_1\), \(APQC\) cyclic gives \(\angle PAC = \angle PQC\) (inscribed angles on arc \(PC\), up to supplement). In \(\omega_2\), \(BPQD\) cyclic gives \(\angle PBD = \angle PQD\). Since \(C,Q,D\) are collinear, \(\angle PQC\) and \(\angle PQD\) are the same angle, so \(\angle PAC = \angle PBD\). These are corresponding angles cut by the transversal line \(APB\) on lines \(AC\) and \(BD\); equal corresponding angles force \(AC \parallel BD\).
problems.verify_note: Numeric: 5 random configurations of two intersecting circles with random lines through P and Q; cross product of direction(AC) and direction(BD) was 0 to 1e-7 in every trial, confirming AC || BD.

problems.statement: Let \(AB\) be a diameter of a circle \(\omega\) with center \(O\), and let \(P\) be a point outside \(\omega\) on the line \(AB\) extended beyond \(B\). From \(P\) draw a tangent \(PT\) to \(\omega\) (with \(T\) the point of tangency), and let \(M\) be the foot of the perpendicular from \(T\) to line \(AB\). Prove that \(PM\cdot PO = PT^{2}\), and deduce that \(M\) is the same point for both tangents from \(P\).
problems.solution:
Set up the standard right-angle facts. Since \(PT\) is tangent at \(T\), the radius \(OT \perp PT\), so triangle \(OTP\) is right-angled at \(T\). The point \(M\) is the foot of the perpendicular from \(T\) onto the hypotenuse \(OP\) of this right triangle (note \(M\) lies on line \(AB = OP\)).\par This is exactly the altitude-on-hypotenuse configuration. In a right triangle, the foot of the altitude from the right angle satisfies the well-known relation: a leg squared equals the product of the hypotenuse and the adjacent segment of the hypotenuse. Applying it to right triangle \(OTP\) with altitude \(TM\):
\[
PT^2 = PM\cdot PO,
\]
since \(PT\) is the leg adjacent to the segment \(PM\). (Equivalently, \(\triangle PTM \sim \triangle POT\): they share angle \(P\) and each has a right angle, giving \(\tfrac{PT}{PO} = \tfrac{PM}{PT}\), i.e. \(PT^2 = PM\cdot PO\).) This proves the stated identity.\par Now deduce the independence from the choice of tangent. The power of \(P\) with respect to \(\omega\) is
\[
PT^2 = \operatorname{pow}(P) = PA\cdot PB,
\]
a quantity determined by \(P\) and \(\omega\) alone, the same for both tangents \(PT\) and \(PT'\). From the identity,
\[
PM = \frac{PT^2}{PO} = \frac{\operatorname{pow}(P)}{PO},
\]
which likewise depends only on \(P\) and \(\omega\), not on which tangent we chose. Both feet \(M\) and \(M'\) lie on the fixed line \(AB\), on the same side of \(P\), at the identical distance \(PM\) from \(P\); hence they coincide, \(M = M'\). So both tangent points project to the same foot \(M\) on \(AB\). \(\blacksquare\)
problems.method2: Coordinate confirmation. Put \(O=(0,0)\), radius \(1\), \(A=(-1,0)\), \(B=(1,0)\), and \(P=(p,0)\) with \(p>1\). The tangent point is \(T=\left(\tfrac1p,\ \pm\tfrac{\sqrt{p^2-1}}{p}\right)\) (it lies on \(\omega\) and satisfies \(OT\perp PT\)). Its foot on the \(x\)-axis is \(M=\left(\tfrac1p,0\right)\), the same \(x\)-coordinate for either sign, so \(M\) is independent of the tangent. Then \(PM = p-\tfrac1p\) and \(PO = p\), so \(PM\cdot PO = p^2-1\); and \(PT^2 = \left(p-\tfrac1p\right)^2 + \tfrac{p^2-1}{p^2} = p^2-1\). The two agree, confirming \(PM\cdot PO = PT^2\).
problems.verify_note: sympy symbolic: with O at origin, radius 1, P=(p,0), got PT^2 = p^2-1 and PM*PO = p^2-1 identically; M=(1/p,0) independent of tangent sign.

problems.statement: Let \(H\) be the orthocenter of an acute triangle \(ABC\), and let \(H'\) be the reflection of \(H\) over the line \(BC\). Prove that \(H'\) lies on the circumcircle of \(ABC\). Then, in the specific triangle with \(A=(0,5)\), \(B=(-3,0)\), \(C=(4,0)\), find the coordinates of \(H'\) and verify it lies on the circumcircle.
problems.solution:
Synthetic proof. We show \(\angle BH'C = 180^\circ - \angle BAC\), which places \(H'\) on the arc \(BC\) opposite \(A\), i.e. on the circumcircle.\par First, the standard orthocenter fact \(\angle BHC = 180^\circ - \angle BAC\). Proof by angle chasing: let \(E\) be the foot of the altitude from \(B\) (on \(AC\)) and \(F\) the foot from \(C\) (on \(AB\)). In quadrilateral \(AFHE\), the angles at \(F\) and \(E\) are right angles, so the remaining two angles sum to \(180^\circ\): \(\angle FHE = 180^\circ - \angle A\). Since \(\angle BHC = \angle FHE\) (vertical angles at \(H\)), we get \(\angle BHC = 180^\circ - \angle A\).\par Reflection over line \(BC\) is an isometry fixing \(B\) and \(C\), so it preserves the angle subtended at the reflected point:
\[
\angle BH'C = \angle BHC = 180^\circ - \angle BAC.
\]
Thus \(\angle BH'C + \angle BAC = 180^\circ\). Since \(H\) is interior to the acute triangle, its reflection \(H'\) lies on the opposite side of line \(BC\) from \(A\). The locus of points on that far side of chord \(BC\) which subtend an angle supplementary to \(\angle BAC\) is precisely the far arc of the circumcircle of \(ABC\). Therefore \(H'\) lies on the circumcircle. \(\blacksquare\)\par Concrete computation for \(A=(0,5),\ B=(-3,0),\ C=(4,0)\). Side \(BC\) is the \(x\)-axis. The altitude from \(A\) is perpendicular to \(BC\), hence the vertical line \(x=0\). The altitude from \(B\) is perpendicular to \(AC\); the direction of \(AC\) is \(C-A=(4,-5)\), so this altitude through \(B=(-3,0)\) satisfies \(4(x+3) - 5y = 0\). Intersecting with \(x=0\): \(4(3)-5y=0\), so \(y=\tfrac{12}{5}\). Thus
\[
H = \left(0,\ \tfrac{12}{5}\right).
\]
Reflecting over \(BC\) (the \(x\)-axis) flips the sign of \(y\):
\[
H' = \left(0,\ -\tfrac{12}{5}\right).
\]
The circumcircle through \(A,B,C\) is \(x^2+y^2+Dx+Ey+F=0\); substituting the three vertices gives \(D=-1,\ E=-\tfrac{13}{5},\ F=-12\), i.e. \(x^2+y^2-x-\tfrac{13}{5}y-12=0\). Testing \(H'=(0,-\tfrac{12}{5})\):
\[
0 + \tfrac{144}{25} - 0 - \tfrac{13}{5}\!\left(-\tfrac{12}{5}\right) - 12 = \tfrac{144}{25} + \tfrac{156}{25} - 12 = \tfrac{300}{25} - 12 = 12-12 = 0.
\]
So \(H'\) satisfies the circle equation and indeed lies on the circumcircle.
problems.method2: Vector/reflection proof. Place the circumcenter \(O\) at the origin with circumradius \(R\); then Euler's identity gives the orthocenter \(H = A+B+C\). Let \(A^* = -A\) be the antipode of \(A\), which lies on the circle. The midpoint \(M\) of \(BC\) satisfies \(2M = B+C = H - A = H + A^*\), so \(A^*\) is the reflection of \(H\) over the point \(M\). Reflecting \(H\) over the line \(BC\) (rather than over the point \(M\)) produces a point at the same distance from \(O\) as \(A^*\), namely \(R\); hence \(|OH'| = R\) and \(H'\) lies on the circumcircle. The explicit coordinates above confirm \(|OH'| = R\) numerically.
problems.verify_note: Verified H=(0,12/5) is orthocenter (BH.AC=0, CH.AB=0); circumcircle solved to D=-1, E=-13/5, F=-12; plugging H'=(0,-12/5) into the circle equation gives 0 exactly.

blurb: Eight original olympiad-flavoured combinatorics problems spanning counting, bijections, the pigeonhole principle, extremal set theory, invariants, and combinatorial games. Each rewards a structural insight rather than brute computation, and every numeric answer has been independently verified by exhaustive search.

problems.statement: A row of \(12\) lockers is to be painted, exactly \(4\) of them red and the rest grey, so that no two red lockers are adjacent. In how many ways can this be done?
problems.solution: Place the \(8\) grey lockers first; they create \(9\) gaps (including the two ends) into which red lockers may go: \[\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_\,G\,\_.\] Choosing \(4\) of these \(9\) gaps to receive a red locker guarantees that no two reds are adjacent, and every valid arrangement arises this way exactly once. Hence the count is \[\binom{9}{4}=126.\] In general, the number of binary strings of length \(n\) with exactly \(k\) ones and no two ones adjacent is \(\binom{n-k+1}{k}\); here \(\binom{12-4+1}{4}=\binom{9}{4}=126.\)
problems.method2: Bijection: map a valid arrangement, with red positions \(1\le a_1<a_2<a_3<a_4\le 12\) and \(a_{i+1}-a_i\ge 2\), to \(b_i=a_i-(i-1)\). Then \(1\le b_1<b_2<b_3<b_4\le 9\) is an arbitrary \(4\)-subset of \(\{1,\dots,9\}\), and the map is a bijection. So the count is \(\binom{9}{4}=126.\)
problems.verify_note: Brute force over all 4-subsets of 12 positions with the non-adjacency constraint gives 126, matching C(9,4)=126.

problems.statement: Let \(S=\{1,2,3,\dots,12\}\). Counting the empty set (whose sum is \(0\)), how many subsets of \(S\) have a sum divisible by \(3\)?
problems.solution: The total over all subsets is encoded by \(\prod_{k=1}^{12}(1+x^{k})\); we want the sum of coefficients of \(x^m\) with \(3\mid m\). Apply the roots-of-unity filter with \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}\): the desired number is \[N=\frac{1}{3}\sum_{j=0}^{2}\prod_{k=1}^{12}\bigl(1+\omega^{jk}\bigr).\] For \(j=0\) the product is \(2^{12}=4096\). For \(j=1\), group the exponents in consecutive triples; over each triple \(\{3t+1,3t+2,3t+3\}\) we get \[(1+\omega)(1+\omega^{2})(1+\omega^{3})=(1+\omega)(1+\omega^{2})\cdot 2.\] Since \(1+\omega+\omega^{2}=0\) and \(\omega^{3}=1\), \((1+\omega)(1+\omega^{2})=1+\omega+\omega^{2}+\omega^{3}=1\), so each triple contributes \(2\). With \(12/3=4\) triples the product is \(2^{4}=16\). The \(j=2\) product is the complex conjugate, also \(16\). Hence \[N=\frac{4096+16+16}{3}=\frac{4128}{3}=1376.\]
problems.method2: Residue bookkeeping. The set \(S\) splits into residue classes mod \(3\): \(\{3,6,9,12\}\) (residue \(0\)), \(\{1,4,7,10\}\) (residue \(1\)), \(\{2,5,8,11\}\) (residue \(2\)), four elements each. The four residue-\(0\) elements may be included freely, contributing a factor \(2^{4}=16\). For the residue-\(1\) class, if we pick \(k_1\) of its elements the sum contributes \(k_1 \pmod 3\); for the residue-\(2\) class, picking \(k_2\) elements contributes \(2k_2 \pmod 3\). The total sum is divisible by \(3\) exactly when \(k_1+2k_2\equiv 0 \pmod 3\). Summing \(\binom{4}{k_1}\binom{4}{k_2}\) over all \(0\le k_1,k_2\le 4\) with \(k_1+2k_2\equiv 0\pmod 3\) gives \(86\) such weighted choices. Multiplying by the free factor: \(16\cdot 86=1376.\)
problems.verify_note: Exhaustive enumeration over all 2^12 subsets of {1..12} gives 1376 with sum divisible by 3. Roots-of-unity value (4096+16+16)/3=1376 confirmed; residue-class method recomputed cleanly to 16*86=1376 (original method2 had a garbled passage, now rewritten).

problems.statement: An ant starts at the bottom-left corner of a \(6\times 4\) city grid and walks to the top-right corner, moving only one block East or one block North at a time. How many distinct shortest routes are there?
problems.solution: A shortest route uses exactly \(6\) East steps and \(4\) North steps, in some order, for a total of \(10\) steps. A route is determined precisely by which \(4\) of the \(10\) step-slots are North (equivalently which \(6\) are East). Therefore the number of routes is \[\binom{10}{4}=\binom{10}{6}=210.\] The key insight is that each monotone path corresponds bijectively to a word in \(\{E,N\}\) with \(6\) E's and \(4\) N's, so counting paths is counting such words.
problems.method2: Build a Pascal-style table: write at each lattice point the number of monotone paths reaching it from the start, where each interior value is the sum of the value below it and the value to its left (boundary cells all equal \(1\)). Filling the \(7\times 5\) array of lattice points propagates Pascal's rule and yields \(210\) at the top-right corner.
problems.verify_note: C(10,4)=C(10,6)=210 confirmed numerically.

problems.statement: Seven distinct prizes are to be handed out to three children named A, B, C, with each child receiving at least one prize. In how many ways can the prizes be distributed?
problems.solution: We count surjections from the \(7\) prizes onto the \(3\) children. Without the 'at least one' restriction there are \(3^{7}=2187\) distributions. By inclusion–exclusion, subtract those missing at least one child: \[\sum_{i=0}^{3}(-1)^{i}\binom{3}{i}(3-i)^{7}=3^{7}-\binom{3}{1}2^{7}+\binom{3}{2}1^{7}-\binom{3}{3}0^{7}.\] This equals \(2187-3\cdot128+3\cdot1-0=2187-384+3=1806.\) The insight is that 'every child nonempty' is exactly the surjection condition, handled cleanly by inclusion–exclusion over which children are excluded.
problems.method2: Via Stirling numbers: the number of ways to split \(7\) distinct prizes into \(3\) nonempty unlabelled groups is the Stirling number \(S(7,3)=301\); assigning the three groups to the three named children multiplies by \(3!=6\), giving \(301\cdot 6=1806.\)
problems.verify_note: Brute force over all 3^7 assignments counting those hitting all three children gives 1806; inclusion-exclusion and 6*S(7,3)=6*301=1806 both match.

problems.statement: A frog starts at \(0\) on the number line and makes \(12\) jumps, each of size \(1\) either right (\(+1\)) or left (\(-1\)). It must finish back at \(0\) and must never be at a negative coordinate at any moment. How many such sequences of \(12\) jumps are possible?
problems.solution: Record each jump as \(+1\) or \(-1\); a valid trip is a sequence of \(2n=12\) steps (so \(n=6\)) with \(n\) plus-steps and \(n\) minus-steps whose every partial sum is \(\ge 0\). These are exactly the Dyck paths of semilength \(n\), counted by the Catalan number \[C_{n}=\frac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}.\] For \(n=6\), \[C_{6}=\frac{1}{7}\binom{12}{6}=\frac{924}{7}=132.\] The insight is recognising the 'never negative, returns to start' walk as a Dyck path.
problems.method2: Reflection principle: among the \(\binom{12}{6}=924\) sequences with six \(+1\)s and six \(-1\)s, count the bad ones that dip to \(-1\). Reflecting the path after its first visit to \(-1\) bijects the bad sequences with all sequences having \(7\) minus-steps and \(5\) plus-steps, of which there are \(\binom{12}{5}=792\). Hence valid \(=924-792=132.\)
problems.verify_note: Brute force over all 2^12 sign sequences counting those returning to 0 and never going negative gives 132 = Catalan C_6 = 924/7; reflection 924-792=132 confirmed.

problems.statement: The numbers \(1,2,3,\dots,2025\) are written on a board. Repeatedly, one erases any two numbers \(a,b\) and writes their absolute difference \(|a-b|\) in their place, reducing the count by one each time. After \(2024\) such operations a single number remains. Prove that this final number cannot be \(0\), and determine whether it must be odd or even.
problems.answer: \text{The final number is always odd; in particular it cannot be } 0. (\text{Proof})
problems.solution: Consider the parity of the sum of all numbers currently on the board. When \(a\) and \(b\) are replaced by \(|a-b|\), the sum changes by \[|a-b|-(a+b).\] Since \(|a-b|\) and \(a+b\) have the same parity (they differ by \(2\min(a,b)\), an even number), the sum's parity is unchanged by every operation. Thus the parity of the total is an invariant. Initially the total is \[1+2+\cdots+2025=\frac{2025\cdot 2026}{2}=2025\cdot 1013=2051325,\] which is odd. Therefore at every stage the sum is odd, and in particular the final lone number equals that odd sum and is odd. An odd number is never \(0\), so \(0\) can never be the result. \(\blacksquare\)
problems.method2: Work modulo \(2\) throughout: replacing \(a,b\) by \(|a-b|\equiv a+b \pmod 2\) is, mod \(2\), the same as replacing two bits by their sum, which preserves the total mod \(2\). The number of odd values among \(1,\dots,2025\) is \(1013\), which is odd, so the parity of the sum is \(1\); this parity is conserved, forcing an odd final value.
problems.verify_note: Sum 1..2025 = 2051325 (odd), and 300 random simulations of the full process always ended on an odd value, confirming the parity invariant and the odd/nonzero conclusion. Odd-value count = 1013 confirmed.

problems.statement: Two players alternately remove stones from a single pile, the player who removes the last stone wins. On each turn a player must remove exactly \(1\), \(3\), or \(4\) stones. Starting from a pile of \(n\) stones with optimal play, determine exactly the values of \(n\) for which the player who moves first loses, and prove your characterization.
problems.answer: \text{First player loses exactly when } n\equiv 0 \text{ or } 2 \pmod 7. (\text{Proof})
problems.solution:
Call a position a P-position (previous player wins, i.e. the player to move loses) or an N-position (next player to move wins). We claim \(n\) is a P-position iff \(n\equiv 0\) or \(2 \pmod 7\). Proof by strong induction on \(n\), using two facts: a position is P iff every move leads to an N-position; it is N iff some move leads to a P-position.

Base cases (\(0\le n\le 6\)): \(n=0\) is a P-position (no move; the player to move has already lost). \(n=1,3,4\) are N (remove all stones and win). \(n=2\): the only legal moves are to remove \(1\) (leaving \(1\), an N-position) — \(3,4\) are too big — so every move leads to N, making \(2\) a P-position. \(n=5\): remove \(3\) to leave \(2\) (P), so N. \(n=6\): remove \(4\) to leave \(2\) (P), so N. This matches the claim on \(0,\dots,6\): P exactly at \(0,2\).

Inductive step (\(n\ge 7\)): suppose the claim holds for all smaller values. The positions reachable from \(n\) are \(n-1,n-3,n-4\). Compute residues mod \(7\):
• If \(n\equiv 0\): the moves reach residues \(6,4,3\), none of which is \(0\) or \(2\); so all moves land in N-positions, hence \(n\) is a P-position. \(\checkmark\)
• If \(n\equiv 2\): moves reach residues \(1,6,5\), again none equal to \(0\) or \(2\); all land in N, so \(n\) is P. \(\checkmark\)
• If \(n\equiv 1\): removing \(1\) reaches residue \(0\) (a P-position), so \(n\) is N. 
• If \(n\equiv 3\): removing \(1\) reaches residue \(2\) (P), so N. 
• If \(n\equiv 4\): removing \(4\) reaches residue \(0\) (P), so N. 
• If \(n\equiv 5\): removing \(3\) reaches residue \(2\) (P), so N. 
• If \(n\equiv 6\): removing \(4\) reaches residue \(2\) (P), so N. 
In every non-\(\{0,2\}\) residue there is a move into a P-position, so those are N. This completes the induction: the first player loses precisely when \(n\equiv 0\) or \(2 \pmod 7\). \( lacksquare\)
problems.method2: Sprague–Grundy: define \(g(n)=\operatorname{mex}\{g(n-1),g(n-3),g(n-4)\}\) where moves to negative piles are unavailable. Computing \(g\) shows it is periodic with period \(7\), and the zero values (losing positions) occur exactly at \(n\equiv 0,2 \pmod 7\); a position is losing iff its Grundy value is \(0\).
problems.verify_note: Direct game-theory DP for n up to 200 gives P-positions exactly at residues {0,2} mod 7, confirming the characterization.

problems.statement: Let \(n\ge 1\). Prove that any \(n+1\) integers chosen from \(\{1,2,3,\dots,2n\}\) must contain two distinct numbers that are coprime, and show that the bound \(n+1\) is best possible (i.e. there is a choice of \(n\) numbers with no two coprime).
problems.solution: Partition \(\{1,2,\dots,2n\}\) into the \(n\) consecutive pairs \[\{1,2\},\{3,4\},\{5,6\},\dots,\{2n-1,2n\}.\] If we select \(n+1\) numbers, then by the pigeonhole principle (\(n+1\) objects into \(n\) boxes) two of the chosen numbers fall in the same pair \(\{2k-1,2k\}\). Two consecutive integers \(2k-1\) and \(2k\) are always coprime, since any common divisor of them divides their difference \(1\); hence \(\gcd(2k-1,2k)=1\). Thus the selection contains two coprime numbers.\n\nSharpness: the set of the \(n\) even numbers \(\{2,4,6,\dots,2n\}\subseteq\{1,\dots,2n\}\) has size \(n\), and any two of its elements share the common factor \(2\), so no two are coprime. Therefore \(n\) numbers need not contain a coprime pair, and the threshold \(n+1\) cannot be lowered. \(\blacksquare\)
problems.method2: Alternative tight construction and counting view: one might try to take \(n\) numbers all sharing a fixed prime divisor; in \(\{1,\dots,2n\}\) the prime \(2\) furnishes exactly \(n\) multiples \(\{2,4,\dots,2n\}\), the largest such pairwise-non-coprime block. Any prime \(p\ge 3\) has only \(\lfloor 2n/p\rfloor<n\) multiples in the range, so no class beats the evens. Hence the maximum size of a 'pairwise non-coprime' subset is exactly \(n\), and \(n+1\) selections force a coprime pair.
problems.verify_note: Exhaustively checked n=1..8: every (n+1)-subset of {1..2n} contains a coprime pair, and the n even numbers form a coprime-free set, confirming both the claim and sharpness.

blurb: Eight original olympiad-style trigonometry problems spanning conditional identities, telescoping trig series, the bounded-amplitude principle for max/min, and the deep symmetry of regular-7-gon angles. Each is built so the decisive move is a structural insight — a clever pairing, a telescope, a Chebyshev/root-of-unity argument, or a convexity bound — rather than brute computation.

problems.statement: Evaluate \[ S=\sin^2 10^\circ+\sin^2 50^\circ+\sin^2 70^\circ. \]
problems.solution:
Use power reduction \(\sin^2\theta=\tfrac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}\):
\[ S=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\bigl(\cos 20^\circ+\cos 100^\circ+\cos 140^\circ\bigr). \]
The insight is to pair the last two by sum-to-product about their mean \(120^\circ\): \(\cos 100^\circ+\cos 140^\circ=2\cos 120^\circ\cos 20^\circ=-\cos 20^\circ\). Hence
\[ \cos 20^\circ+\cos 100^\circ+\cos 140^\circ=\cos 20^\circ-\cos 20^\circ=0, \]
and therefore \(S=\dfrac{3}{2}.\)
problems.method2: Complex-exponential view: the doubled angles can be arranged so that \(\operatorname{Re}\bigl(e^{i\,20^\circ}+e^{i\,100^\circ}+e^{i\,140^\circ}\bigr)\) is computed via \(\cos100^\circ+\cos140^\circ=-\cos20^\circ\), giving \(0\); thus the cosine sum vanishes and \(S=\tfrac32\).
problems.verify_note: sympy: simplify(S)=3/2 exactly (float 1.5).

problems.statement: Without a calculator, prove that \[ \tan 20^\circ\,\tan 40^\circ\,\tan 80^\circ=\tan 60^\circ, \] and state the numerical value.
problems.solution: The hidden engine is the factorization \(\tan 3\theta=\tan\theta\,\tan(60^\circ-\theta)\,\tan(60^\circ+\theta)\). With \(\theta=20^\circ\), \(60^\circ-\theta=40^\circ\) and \(60^\circ+\theta=80^\circ\), so \(\tan 60^\circ=\tan 20^\circ\tan 40^\circ\tan 80^\circ\). To justify the identity, write \(\tan 3\theta=\tan\theta\cdot\dfrac{3-\tan^2\theta}{1-3\tan^2\theta}\). With \(t=\tan\theta\), the factorizations \(3-t^2=(\sqrt3-t)(\sqrt3+t)\) and \(1-3t^2=(1-\sqrt3 t)(1+\sqrt3 t)\), together with \(\tan(60^\circ\mp\theta)=\dfrac{\sqrt3\mp t}{1\pm\sqrt3 t}\), give \(\tan(60^\circ-\theta)\tan(60^\circ+\theta)=\dfrac{3-t^2}{1-3t^2}=\dfrac{\tan3\theta}{\tan\theta}\). Multiplying by \(\tan\theta\) proves the claim. Since \(\tan 60^\circ=\sqrt3\), the product is \(\sqrt3\).
problems.method2: Direct evaluation uses \(\sin 20^\circ\sin 40^\circ\sin 80^\circ=\tfrac{\sqrt3}{8}\) and \(\cos 20^\circ\cos 40^\circ\cos 80^\circ=\tfrac18\); their ratio is the desired product, equal to \(\sqrt3\).

problems.statement: Find the exact set of values taken by \[ f(x)=12\sin x-5\cos x+4,\qquad x\in\mathbb{R}, \] and the values of \(x\) where the maximum is attained.
problems.solution: Any \(a\sin x+b\cos x\) is a single sinusoid of amplitude \(\sqrt{a^2+b^2}\): there is a phase \(\varphi\) with \(a\sin x+b\cos x=\sqrt{a^2+b^2}\sin(x+\varphi)\). Here \(a=12,b=-5\), amplitude \(\sqrt{144+25}=13\), so \(f(x)=13\sin(x+\varphi)+4\) with \(\tan\varphi=-\tfrac{5}{12}\). Since \(\sin(x+\varphi)\in[-1,1]\), \(f\) ranges over \([4-13,4+13]=[-9,17]\). The maximum \(17\) occurs when \(\sin(x+\varphi)=1\); at that point \((\sin x,\cos x)=\bigl(\tfrac{12}{13},-\tfrac{5}{13}\bigr)\), i.e. \(x=\pi-\arctan\tfrac{12}{5}\) (second quadrant), plus integer multiples of \(2\pi\).
problems.method2: Calculus: \(f'(x)=12\cos x+5\sin x=0\Rightarrow\tan x=-\tfrac{12}{5}\); the two critical directions give \((\sin x,\cos x)=\pm\bigl(\tfrac{12}{13},-\tfrac{5}{13}\bigr)\), hence \(f=4\pm13=\{17,-9\}\), and by continuity the range is the whole interval \([-9,17]\).
problems.verify_note: numpy sweep: min=-9.000, max=17.000; max attained at x=pi-arctan(12/5)=1.9656 with f=17 exactly.

problems.statement: Let \(\theta\) be such that none of \(\theta,2\theta,\dots,2^n\theta\) is a multiple of \(\pi\). Find a closed form for \[ T_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{\sin\!\left(2^{k}\theta\right)} =\frac{1}{\sin 2\theta}+\frac{1}{\sin 4\theta}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\sin 2^{n}\theta}. \]
problems.solution:
The decisive observation is that a cosecant of a doubled angle is a difference of cotangents:
\[ \cot\alpha-\cot 2\alpha=\frac{\cos\alpha}{\sin\alpha}-\frac{\cos 2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha}=\frac{2\cos^2\alpha-\cos 2\alpha}{\sin 2\alpha}=\frac{2\cos^2\alpha-(2\cos^2\alpha-1)}{\sin 2\alpha}=\frac{1}{\sin 2\alpha}. \]
Setting \(\alpha=2^{k-1}\theta\) gives \(\dfrac{1}{\sin 2^k\theta}=\cot 2^{k-1}\theta-\cot 2^{k}\theta\). Summing for \(k=1,\dots,n\) telescopes:
\[ T_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\bigl(\cot 2^{k-1}\theta-\cot 2^{k}\theta\bigr)=\cot\theta-\cot 2^{n}\theta. \]
problems.method2: Induction: base \(n=1\) is \(\csc 2\theta=\cot\theta-\cot 2\theta\). Step: \(T_{n+1}=(\cot\theta-\cot 2^n\theta)+(\cot 2^n\theta-\cot 2^{n+1}\theta)=\cot\theta-\cot 2^{n+1}\theta.\)
problems.verify_note: sympy: T_n-(cot theta-cot(2^n theta))=0 for n=1,2,3,5 at theta=0.07.

problems.statement: For \(0<\theta<\pi\) prove the product formula \[ \prod_{k=1}^{n}\cos\!\frac{\theta}{2^{k}}=\frac{\sin\theta}{\,2^{n}\sin(\theta/2^{n})\,}, \] and deduce the value of the infinite product \(\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\cos\frac{\theta}{2^{k}}\).
problems.solution:
Multiply \(P_n=\prod_{k=1}^n\cos(\theta/2^k)\) by the smallest sine \(\sin(\theta/2^n)\) and cascade \(\sin 2x=2\sin x\cos x\):
\[ P_n\sin\frac{\theta}{2^n}=\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\cdots\cos\frac{\theta}{2^{n-1}}\cdot\tfrac12\sin\frac{\theta}{2^{n-1}}. \]
The new sine merges with the preceding cosine, and the collapse repeats \(n\) times to give \(P_n\sin(\theta/2^n)=\tfrac{1}{2^{n}}\sin\theta\), so
\[ P_n=\frac{\sin\theta}{2^{n}\sin(\theta/2^{n})}. \]
For the infinite product, \(2^n\sin(\theta/2^n)=\theta\cdot\dfrac{\sin(\theta/2^n)}{\theta/2^n}\to\theta\), hence \(\prod_{k=1}^{\infty}\cos\frac{\theta}{2^k}=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\theta}\). (At \(\theta=\pi/2\) this is Viète's formula \(\tfrac{2}{\pi}=\cos\tfrac{\pi}{4}\cos\tfrac{\pi}{8}\cdots\).)
problems.method2: Induction: assuming \(P_{n-1}=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{2^{n-1}\sin(\theta/2^{n-1})}\), substitute \(\sin(\theta/2^{n-1})=2\sin(\theta/2^n)\cos(\theta/2^n)\) into \(P_n=P_{n-1}\cos(\theta/2^n)\) to cancel the cosine and obtain \(P_n=\dfrac{\sin\theta}{2^n\sin(\theta/2^n)}\).
problems.verify_note: sympy: P_n - sin(theta)/(2^n sin(theta/2^n))=0 for n=1,2,4,6; numeric infinite product at theta=1.23 matches sin(theta)/theta=0.76625.

problems.statement: Prove that \[ \cos\frac{\pi}{7}-\cos\frac{2\pi}{7}+\cos\frac{3\pi}{7}=\frac{1}{2}. \]
problems.solution:
First reduce to a positive-sign sum. Since \(\cos\frac{5\pi}{7}=-\cos\frac{2\pi}{7}\), the target equals \(C:=\cos\frac{\pi}{7}+\cos\frac{3\pi}{7}+\cos\frac{5\pi}{7}\). Now use the seven solutions of \(z^7=-1\), namely \(z=e^{i(2k+1)\pi/7}\), \(k=0,\dots,6\), which sum to \(0\) (coefficient of \(z^6\) in \(z^7+1\) is \(0\)). Their real parts pair as conjugates, and \(k=3\) gives \(\cos\pi=-1\):
\[ 2\Bigl(\cos\tfrac{\pi}{7}+\cos\tfrac{3\pi}{7}+\cos\tfrac{5\pi}{7}\Bigr)+(-1)=0\ \Longrightarrow\ C=\tfrac12. \]
Thus \(\cos\frac{\pi}{7}-\cos\frac{2\pi}{7}+\cos\frac{3\pi}{7}=C=\tfrac12.\)
problems.method2: Multiply \(C\) by \(2\sin\frac{\pi}{7}\) and use \(2\sin\frac{\pi}{7}\cos\frac{(2j-1)\pi}{7}=\sin\frac{2j\pi}{7}-\sin\frac{(2j-2)\pi}{7}\); the sum telescopes to \(\sin\frac{6\pi}{7}=\sin\frac{\pi}{7}\), so \(C=\tfrac12\). Then \(\cos\frac{5\pi}{7}=-\cos\frac{2\pi}{7}\) gives the stated form.
problems.verify_note: sympy: simplify of LHS = 1/2 exactly; C also numerically 0.5.

problems.statement: Show that \[ \tan^{2}\frac{\pi}{7}+\tan^{2}\frac{2\pi}{7}+\tan^{2}\frac{3\pi}{7}=21. \]
problems.solution:
Build a polynomial whose roots are exactly the three quantities. Expanding \((\cos\theta+i\sin\theta)^7\) and forming \(\tan 7\theta=\dfrac{\operatorname{Im}}{\operatorname{Re}}\) gives, with \(t=\tan\theta\),
\[ \tan 7\theta=\frac{7t-35t^3+21t^5-t^7}{1-21t^2+35t^4-7t^6}. \]
For \(\theta=\frac{k\pi}{7}\) we have \(\tan 7\theta=0\), so \(t=\tan\frac{k\pi}{7}\) satisfies the numerator: \(t\bigl(t^6-21t^4+35t^2-7\bigr)=0\). The nonzero roots \(\tan\frac{k\pi}{7}\,(k=1,\dots,6)\) occur in \(\pm\) pairs since \(\tan\frac{(7-k)\pi}{7}=-\tan\frac{k\pi}{7}\); hence \(u=t^2\) takes the three values \(\tan^2\frac{\pi}{7},\tan^2\frac{2\pi}{7},\tan^2\frac{3\pi}{7}\), the roots of
\[ u^{3}-21u^{2}+35u-7=0. \]
By Vieta the sum of roots is the negation of the \(u^2\)-coefficient, namely \(21\). Hence the sum of squares is \(21\). (Bonus: \(\sum\sec^2\frac{k\pi}{7}=21+3=24\).)
problems.method2: Derive the same cubic in \(u=\tan^2\) from \(\cos 7\theta=0\) at \(\theta=\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{14}\) (cotangent version) and read the leading coefficients; Vieta again yields sum \(=21\).
problems.verify_note: sympy: simplify of tan^2 sum = 21 exactly; sec^2 bonus = 24 confirmed.

problems.statement: Let \(A,B,C\) be the angles of a triangle, so \(A+B+C=\pi\) and \(A,B,C\in(0,\pi)\). Determine the minimum value of \[ g=\cot^{2}A+\cot^{2}B+\cot^{2}C, \] and prove it is the minimum.
problems.solution:
At the equilateral triangle \(A=B=C=\frac{\pi}{3}\), \(\cot\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt3}\), so \(g=3\cdot\tfrac13=1\). We prove \(g\ge1\). The key tool is the triangle identity
\[ \cot A\cot B+\cot B\cot C+\cot C\cot A=1, \]
which follows from \(\cot C=-\cot(A+B)=\dfrac{1-\cot A\cot B}{\cot A+\cot B}\) after clearing denominators. Combine it with the SOS inequality \(x^2+y^2+z^2\ge xy+yz+zx\) (equivalently \(\tfrac12\sum(x-y)^2\ge0\)) at \(x=\cot A,y=\cot B,z=\cot C\):
\[ \cot^2A+\cot^2B+\cot^2C\ge \cot A\cot B+\cot B\cot C+\cot C\cot A=1. \]
Equality needs \(\cot A=\cot B=\cot C\), i.e. \(A=B=C=\frac{\pi}{3}\), which is admissible. Hence the minimum value is \(1\). (The expression is unbounded above as any angle \(\to0\), so only a minimum exists.)
problems.method2: Lagrange multipliers on \(\sum\cot^2A\) subject to \(\sum A=\pi\) give \(\cot A\csc^2A=\cot B\csc^2B=\cot C\csc^2C\); the unique interior solution is \(A=B=C=\frac{\pi}{3}\), yielding \(g=1\), confirmed as the minimum by the boundary blow-up.
problems.verify_note: 2M-sample numeric search over triangles: min approx 1.0000 attained near (60,60,60) degrees; identity cotAcotB+...=1 underpins the SOS bound.

blurb: An original eight-problem ladder through real-valued functional equations: Cauchy additivity, the swap and reciprocal-cycle substitution tricks, exploiting injectivity and surjectivity, and pinning down functions via fixed points. Each problem turns on a single decisive idea rather than computation, ordered from warm-up to hard olympiad.

problems.statement: A function \( f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} \) satisfies \( f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y) \) for all real \( x,y \), and \( f(3)=12 \). Find \( f(10) \), and show this value is forced even without assuming \( f \) is continuous.
problems.solution: Set \( x=y=0 \): \( f(0)=2f(0) \), so \( f(0)=0 \). For any real \( a \) and positive integer \( n \), repeated use of additivity gives \( f(na)=nf(a) \) by induction: \( f((n+1)a)=f(na)+f(a)=nf(a)+f(a)=(n+1)f(a) \). Apply with \( a=1,\ n=3 \): \( 12=f(3)=f(1+1+1)=3f(1) \Rightarrow f(1)=4 \). Then \( f(10)=f(10\cdot 1)=10f(1)=40. \) No continuity is needed: the value at any integer multiple of a base point is determined purely by additivity, and \( 10 \) is an integer multiple of \( 1 \). Hence \( f(10)=40 \) is forced. (The general additive solution can be wildly discontinuous, but it is still pinned down at every rational, so all integer values are determined.)
problems.verify_note: sympy/arith confirmed f(1)=4, f(10)=40.

problems.statement: Find all functions \( f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} \) satisfying \[ f(x)-2f(2-x)=x^{2}\qquad\text{for all } x\in\mathbb{R}, \] and compute \( f(0) \).
problems.solution: The substitution \( x\mapsto 2-x \) is an involution, since \( 2-(2-x)=x \); applying it twice returns to the start. Write the given equation and its image under \( x\mapsto 2-x \): \[ f(x)-2f(2-x)=x^{2}\tag{A} \] \[ f(2-x)-2f(x)=(2-x)^{2}.\tag{B} \] Treat \( f(x) \) and \( f(2-x) \) as two unknowns. From (A), \( f(x)=x^{2}+2f(2-x) \). Substitute into (B): \[ f(2-x)-2\big(x^{2}+2f(2-x)\big)=(2-x)^{2}\ \Rightarrow\ -3f(2-x)=(2-x)^{2}+2x^{2}, \] so \( f(2-x)=-\dfrac{(2-x)^{2}+2x^{2}}{3} \). Then \[ f(x)=x^{2}+2f(2-x)=x^{2}-\tfrac{2}{3}\big((2-x)^{2}+2x^{2}\big). \] Expanding, \( (2-x)^2+2x^2=3x^2-4x+4 \), so \( f(x)=x^2-\tfrac{2}{3}(3x^2-4x+4)=-x^{2}+\dfrac{8x}{3}-\dfrac{8}{3} \). A quick check confirms (A). Hence \( f(0)=-\dfrac{8}{3} \). The solution is unique because the \( 2\times2 \) linear system has determinant \( (1)(1)-(-2)(-2)=-3\neq0 \).
problems.method2: Ansatz: since the right side is quadratic and the transformation is affine, seek \( f(x)=ax^{2}+bx+c \). Then \( f(x)-2f(2-x)=ax^2+bx+c-2\big(a(2-x)^2+b(2-x)+c\big) \). Matching the \( x^2 \) coefficient gives \( a-2a=1\Rightarrow a=-1 \); matching the \( x \)- and constant coefficients then yields \( b=\tfrac{8}{3},\ c=-\tfrac{8}{3} \), the same function.
problems.verify_note: sympy solved the 2x2 system: f(x)=-x^2+8x/3-8/3, f(0)=-8/3; original eq residual simplifies to 0.

problems.statement: A function \( f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} \) satisfies \[ f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)+xy(x+y)\qquad\text{for all }x,y\in\mathbb{R}, \] and \( f(1)=1 \). Find \( f(3) \).
problems.solution: The cubic-looking term \( xy(x+y) \) is exactly the obstruction produced by a cubic. Note \( (x+y)^3=x^3+y^3+3xy(x+y) \), so \( p(x)=\tfrac{1}{3}x^{3} \) satisfies \[ p(x+y)-p(x)-p(y)=\tfrac{1}{3}\big((x+y)^3-x^3-y^3\big)=xy(x+y), \] i.e. \( p \) is a particular solution of the inhomogeneous equation. Set \( g(x)=f(x)-\tfrac{1}{3}x^{3} \). Subtracting the two relations, \[ g(x+y)=\big(f(x)+f(y)+xy(x+y)\big)-\big(p(x)+p(y)+xy(x+y)\big)=g(x)+g(y), \] so \( g \) is additive (Cauchy). Hence \( g(3)=3g(1) \) (additivity forces this at every integer, no continuity needed). Now \( g(1)=f(1)-\tfrac{1}{3}=1-\tfrac{1}{3}=\tfrac{2}{3} \), so \( g(3)=2 \). Therefore \[ f(3)=g(3)+\tfrac{1}{3}\cdot 27=2+9=11. \]
problems.verify_note: Recurrence f(x+1)=f(x)+1+x^2+x gives f(2)=4, f(3)=11; matches g-substitution method.

problems.statement: Find all functions \( f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} \) of the form \( f(x)=cx \) (a real constant \( c \)) for which \[ f\big(f(x)\big)+f(x)=6x\qquad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb{R}. \] For each such \( f \), determine all real fixed points (solutions of \( f(x)=x \)).
problems.answer: \( f(x)=2x \) (fixed point only \( x=0 \)) and \( f(x)=-3x \) (fixed point only \( x=0 \))
problems.solution: With \( f(x)=cx \) we have \( f(f(x))=c(cx)=c^{2}x \), so the equation becomes \( c^{2}x+cx=6x \) for all \( x \), i.e. \[ c^{2}+c-6=0\ \Longrightarrow\ (c-2)(c+3)=0\ \Longrightarrow\ c=2\ \text{or}\ c=-3. \] Thus the linear solutions are exactly \( f(x)=2x \) and \( f(x)=-3x \). The characteristic equation \( c^2+c-6=0 \) is precisely the relation you would get by treating the iterate \( f\circ f \) like a second-order recurrence on the slope. Fixed points: solve \( cx=x \), i.e. \( (c-1)x=0 \). Since \( c=2 \) gives \( c-1=1\neq0 \) and \( c=-3 \) gives \( c-1=-4\neq0 \), in both cases the only solution is \( x=0 \). So each map has the unique fixed point \( x=0 \). (Insight: any such \( f \) must fix \( 0 \): putting \( x=0 \) gives \( f(f(0))+f(0)=0 \), automatic for a linear map; the slope condition then isolates the two admissible rates.)
problems.method2: View the equation as forcing the operator identity \( f^{2}+f-6\,\mathrm{id}=0 \) on the slope, where \( f^2=f\circ f \). Factor: \( (f-2\,\mathrm{id})(f+3\,\mathrm{id})=0 \). For a scalar multiplier \( c \) this annihilator means \( c=2 \) or \( c=-3 \), matching the roots above; the fixed-point set of \( x\mapsto cx \) with \( c\neq1 \) is \( \{0\} \).
problems.verify_note: sympy solved c^2+c-6=0 -> c in {2,-3}; (c-1)x=0 gives x=0 only for both.

problems.statement: Find all functions \( f \) defined on \( \mathbb{R}\setminus\{0,1\} \) satisfying \[ f(x)+f\!\left(\frac{1}{1-x}\right)=x\qquad\text{for all } x\neq 0,1, \] and evaluate \( f(2) \).
problems.solution: The key observation: the substitution \( T(x)=\dfrac{1}{1-x} \) has order three. Compute \( T(T(x))=\dfrac{1}{1-\frac{1}{1-x}}=\dfrac{x-1}{x} \), and \( T(T(T(x)))=x \). So the three points \( x,\ T(x)=\dfrac{1}{1-x},\ T^2(x)=\dfrac{x-1}{x} \) form a 3-cycle, all lying in the domain. Apply the equation at these three arguments: \[ f(x)+f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{1-x}\right)=x,\tag{1} \] \[ f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{1-x}\right)+f\!\left(\tfrac{x-1}{x}\right)=\tfrac{1}{1-x},\tag{2} \] \[ f\!\left(\tfrac{x-1}{x}\right)+f(x)=\tfrac{x-1}{x}.\tag{3} \] Form \( (1)-(2)+(3) \): the middle terms cancel and \( 2f(x)=x-\dfrac{1}{1-x}+\dfrac{x-1}{x} \). Hence \[ f(x)=\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\frac{1}{1-x}+\frac{x-1}{x}\right)=\frac{x^{3}-x+1}{2x(x-1)}. \] This satisfies (1) by construction, and uniqueness follows since the \( 3\times3 \) cyclic system is nonsingular. Finally \( f(2)=\dfrac{8-2+1}{2\cdot2\cdot1}=\dfrac{7}{4} \).
problems.method2: Denote \( a=f(x),\ b=f(T(x)),\ d=f(T^2(x)) \), with right-hand sides \( x,\ \tfrac{1}{1-x},\ \tfrac{x-1}{x} \) summing to \( S \). Adding all three equations gives \( 2(a+b+d)=S \), so \( a+b+d=\tfrac{S}{2} \); subtracting equation (2) (which is \( b+d=\tfrac{1}{1-x} \)) isolates \( a=f(x)=\tfrac{S}{2}-\tfrac{1}{1-x} \). At \( x=2 \), \( S=2-1+\tfrac12=\tfrac32 \), so \( f(2)=\tfrac34-(-1)=\tfrac74 \).
problems.verify_note: sympy: T has order 3 (T^3=x); closed form simplifies to (x^3-x+1)/(2x(x-1)); functional-eq residual = 0; f(2)=7/4.

problems.statement: A continuous function \( f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} \), not identically zero, satisfies \[ f(x)f(y)=f(x+y)+f(x-y)\qquad\text{for all }x,y\in\mathbb{R}. \] Prove that \( f \) is even and that \( f(0)=2 \), and describe all such \( f \).
problems.answer: \( f(0)=2 \); the continuous nonzero solutions are \( f(x)=2\cos(ax) \) and \( f(x)=2\cosh(ax) \) for some real \( a\ge 0 \) (with \( a=0 \) giving \( f\equiv 2 \)).
problems.solution: Step 1 (value at 0). Put \( x=y=0 \): \( f(0)^2=f(0)+f(0)=2f(0) \), so \( f(0)\in\{0,2\} \). If \( f(0)=0 \), set \( y=0 \) in the original: \( f(x)f(0)=f(x)+f(x) \), i.e. \( 0=2f(x) \), forcing \( f\equiv0 \), excluded. Hence \( f(0)=2 \). Step 2 (evenness). Put \( x=0 \): \( f(0)f(y)=f(y)+f(-y) \), i.e. \( 2f(y)=f(y)+f(-y) \), so \( f(-y)=f(y) \); \( f \) is even. Step 3 (classification). The relation \( f(x)f(y)=f(x+y)+f(x-y) \) is d'Alembert's equation (normalized so \( f(0)=2 \)). Write \( g=f/2 \); then \( g(x)g(y)=\tfrac12\big(g(x+y)+g(x-y)\big) \), the standard cosine equation, whose continuous solutions are \( g(x)=\cos(ax) \) or \( g(x)=\cosh(ax) \) for some \( a\ge0 \). Therefore \( f(x)=2\cos(ax) \) or \( f(x)=2\cosh(ax) \); the degenerate \( a=0 \) case is \( f\equiv2 \). Verification: for \( f=2\cos(ax) \), \( f(x)f(y)=4\cos ax\cos ay=2\big(\cos a(x+y)+\cos a(x-y)\big)=f(x+y)+f(x-y) \); the \( \cosh \) case uses \( \cosh u\cosh v=\tfrac12(\cosh(u+v)+\cosh(u-v)) \). Thus all continuous nonzero solutions are exactly these.
problems.method2: Without quoting d'Alembert: evenness plus the substitution \( y=x \) gives \( f(x)^2=f(2x)+f(0)=f(2x)+2 \), a doubling formula \( f(2x)=f(x)^2-2 \) — exactly the identity satisfied by \( 2\cos \) (\( 2\cos2\theta=(2\cos\theta)^2-2 \)) and \( 2\cosh \). Continuity then upgrades determinacy to the closed forms; in all cases \( f(0)=2 \) and \( f \) is even, as required.
problems.verify_note: sympy confirmed 2cos(ax), 2cosh(ax), and const 2 all give f(x)f(y)-f(x+y)-f(x-y)=0 identically; x=y=0 forces f(0) in {0,2}, nonzero forces 2; x=0 gives evenness. Standard d'Alembert classification.

problems.statement: Find all functions \( f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} \) satisfying both \[ f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\quad\text{and}\quad f\big(x^{2}\big)=f(x)^{2}\qquad\text{for all }x,y\in\mathbb{R}. \] (No continuity is assumed.)
problems.solution: Additivity gives \( f(0)=0 \) and \( f(qx)=qf(x) \) for all rationals \( q \); in particular \( f \) restricted to \( \mathbb{Q} \) is \( q\mapsto cq \) with \( c=f(1) \). Step 1 (sign on nonnegatives). For \( t\ge0 \) write \( t=x^2 \); then \( f(t)=f(x^2)=f(x)^2\ge0 \). So \( f \) is nonnegative on \( [0,\infty) \). Step 2 (monotonicity). If \( a\le b \), then \( b-a\ge0 \), so \( f(b)-f(a)=f(b-a)\ge0 \) by additivity and Step 1. Thus \( f \) is nondecreasing. An additive function that is monotone is linear: combined with \( f(q)=cq \) on rationals, monotonicity forces \( f(x)=cx \) for all real \( x \) (squeeze \( x \) between rationals from both sides). Step 3 (pin the slope). The square condition gives \( f(1)=f(1)^2 \), i.e. \( c=c^2 \), so \( c\in\{0,1\} \). Therefore \( f\equiv0 \) or \( f(x)=x \). Both clearly satisfy the two equations. The decisive insight is that the square condition is a hidden positivity/monotonicity hypothesis — it tames the otherwise pathological additive solutions without invoking continuity.
